While one source briefly lists "persimmons" among human foods that may generally cause "intoxications" in pets1, the sources do not detail any specific chemical poison associated with the fruit. Instead of a chemical toxin, the sources emphasize that persimmons pose a severe physical hazard: Persimmon Pits (Seeds) Name of the hazard: Intact Persimmon
chemicals.name: Based on the provided sources, there are no specific chemical toxic compounds or substances identified in persimmons that harm cats. Therefore

description: Ripe persimmon flesh is not acutely toxic to cats, but this fruit presents several practical concerns. The seeds contain cyanogenic glycosides similar to other stone fruit seeds, and the very high tannin content in unripe persimmons causes significant GI upset. The flesh is also high in natural sugars.
emergencyNote: Based on the provided sources, there are no specific veterinary medical procedures
name: Persimmons
safetyNotes: Seeds: contain cyanogenic compounds — remove before offering any flesh
Unripe persimmons: very high tannin content causes severe GI irritation — vomiting, diarrhea, and possible intestinal inflammation
Ripe flesh: safe in very small amounts but high in sugar; no nutritional benefit for cats
Tannins can also cause intestinal obstruction by binding digestive proteins in large quantities
A tiny taste of ripe, seedless flesh is unlikely to cause harm; avoid making it a regular treat

symptoms.name: Persimmons do not contain a specific chemical toxin that causes acute systemic poisoning in cats. Instead
Intestinal Obstruction
symptoms.notes: The pits of persimmons are hard, not easily digested, and are highly prone to causing dangerous physical blockages in the intestinal tracts of cats and dogs12. treatments.name: Based on the provided sources, there are no specific veterinary medical procedures

treatments.description: of the procedure: Contact an animal hospital immediately to inform them of the situation and transport the cat for professional emergency care. You should collect any remaining pieces of the ingested item (such as the persimmon or its pits) in a bag and bring it with you, as this gives the veterinarian a clear basis for making a diagnostic and treatment judgment34. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Emergency Veterinary Consultation and Professional Care
treatments.notes: It is highly emphasized that you must not attempt to induce vomiting at home (such as by administering hydrogen peroxide) before speaking with a veterinarian, as this can be extremely dangerous with obstructive objects. Additionally, do not give the cat milk, food, salt, oil, or any other substances

treatments.description: of the procedure: Before heading to the vet, immediately move the cat away from the location of the food and clean up any remaining pieces to prevent further ingestion. Carefully check the cat's breathing and behavior to see if they are functioning normally34. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Immediate At-Home Triage (Prior to Transport)
treatments.notes: Ensure that children and any other pets in the household are kept away from the scene to prevent them from also ingesting the dangerous item
